success_surface: Sheet-level row filtering with format preservation
effective_behaviors: Explore complete spreadsheet structure before implementation to validate data layout and edge cases
Use filter-based row selection strategy instead of in-place deletion for predictable outcomes
Preserve cell formatting during transformations by explicitly copying font, fill, border, and number_format attributes
Use copy() function when transferring style objects to avoid unhashable type errors
Verify output integrity by reading back saved file and confirming row counts and content
Recover from errors through targeted debugging rather than abandoning the approach
evidence: Agent loaded workbook and printed full content with total row count before filtering logic
Identified rows to keep ([2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9]) separately from rows to exclude
Fixed TypeError by importing 'from copy import copy' and using copy(src_cell.font) pattern
After saving, reloaded output file and iterated through all rows to verify content matches expected filtered result
Output showed correct final row count (7 including header) matching 6 data rows after filtering out STATIONERY/VEGETABLES entries

memory_items.title: Preflight Spreadsheet Exploration
memory_items.lesson: Always load and inspect complete workbook structure before implementing manipulations
memory_items.generalization: When beginning any sheet-level operation, first enumerate headers, row counts, and relevant column values to inform filtering/scope decisions

memory_items.title: Safe Style Object Transfer
memory_items.lesson: Style objects cannot be directly assigned between cells due to unhashable type issues
memory_items.generalization: When copying cell styles via openpyxl, always use Python's copy() function on style attributes (font, fill, border, number_format)

memory_items.title: Filter-Before-Write Pattern
memory_items.lesson: Building output selectively from pre-filtered row indices is more reliable than in-place modifications
memory_items.generalization: For row deletion/filtering tasks, collect indices of rows to keep first, then construct a new worksheet containing only those rows with preserved formatting

memory_items.title: Post-Save Verification Routine
memory_items.lesson: Saving alone does not guarantee correct output; verification confirms both data and formatting integrity
memory_items.generalization: After writing the output file, reload it and iterate through rows/columns to sanity-check row counts, critical cell values, and formatting attributes

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Formatting Preservation During Filtering
- When constructing filtered outputs, never modify rows in-place; instead collect indices to keep and build a new worksheet
- Use `from copy import copy` when assigning style objects (font, fill, border, number_format) between cells to prevent unhashable type errors

### Verification Best Practices
- Always read back the saved file immediately after writing to verify row counts match expected filtered results
- Confirm that skipped rows (based on filtering criteria) are absent from the output

### Error Recovery Pattern
- When encountering StyleProxy or similar openpyxl attribute errors, apply `copy()` to style objects rather than direct assignment
- Iterate through debugging steps methodically rather than restarting the entire approach
